sentences.text: Bernard Presser, born the 16th of September, 1914.
Krakow, Poland.
Not.
No.
Berek was a nickname,
Yes.
I had a brother and a sister.
Sister is still living in Melbourne, and brother pass away in [NON-ENGLISH].
Yanek-- Yanek and his-- [INAUDIBLE] it was-- I can't remember.
Jonas.
Jonas.
Bronislawa.
Frania was my mother, and Chaim was my father name.
He-- they call him [?
Heinrich ?].
My father was professional waiter.
He own-- before the war, a [? big ?] big business.
But he was [? cross with ?] his [? partner ?] [INAUDIBLE] business, he went away.
And after the war, he was in, first of all, called to the military.
But he came back.
In between, I remember that my mother was attending hospital in school, where they [INAUDIBLE] for the hospital.
And they-- she brought some [? programs. ?] I was about five or six.
I still remember it.
She was not a nurse.
She was [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, later my mother was in a business for about 20 years.
They call it Moulin Rouge.
It was nightclub.
And-- but was not very successful, in-- in 1930s, '35.
He went out, penniless, and started a-- a small bistro.
He bought it [INAUDIBLE].
And unfortunately, he passed away a few years later.
Yeah.
Heart-- he just collapsed behind a-- behind the counter.
He was-- was gone.
My mother run the business.
I was-- my-- my brother got married, and after that I opened the business by 7 o'clock, stay until quarter to 9:00.
I had to go to my job at 9 o'clock.
Yes, because I was-- I finish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] business college two years early.
It was four years, but I-- I finished only two years of that.
And I waited and went to work for, in the [INAUDIBLE], it was a kind of hardware.
But all the tools and everything was-- was [INAUDIBLE] before.
Oh, well, that's the 11 subject of all-- was math, accountantcy, bookkeeping, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] French, German, stenography, geography.
Well, I just couldn't-- the Polish, as well.
Literature [? and so on. ?] But my best subject was physics.
Oh, I have plenty of idea.
But I didn't realize that I can do it.
I want to-- to-- because my family-- or rather, my mother's family-- ran a plumber [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] business.
She was, as I remember, she was a widow [INAUDIBLE] 1940-- '21.
She ran [INAUDIBLE] the business.
I like-- like that one.
I was even helping her, sometimes-- running on the [? roofs. ?] [LAUGHS]
No, just-- just-- as a kids like to be, say, policeman or some other things, you know, there's--
Oh, there was plenty.
I have quite a few of them.
After the-- when I was in sixth grade, I met my friends with whom I was very close to, for years and years.
What was their name?
Heinrich ?] [?
Novak-- ?] his name was [?
Friedman. ?] He changed to [?
Novak. ?] [?
Heinrich ?] [?
Freidigger, ?] and [?
Yusef ?] [?
Longsberger, ?] and, well, another one, very-- very-- who-- I just can't remember.
Maybe I remember it later.
OK.
And did they survive the war, your friends?
Only two of them, Novak and [?
Freidigger. ?]
I was very good-- good-- in contact with a non-Jewish friend.
But it was not friend, rather colleagues.
You know?
They-- they-- I invited them to-- to my house.
They invited me over.
We had been playing football-- soccer, rather-- together.
So we-- we-- we are going-- going very well [? with them, ?] [INAUDIBLE].

I remember in high school, when I was, there was-- on the first day of open the school, the University [INAUDIBLE], I was-- I was going with a-- two or three of the Polish colleagues [? when I was ?] [INAUDIBLE].
And they tell me, sit, stay in the-- in the middle of us.
Nobody will touch us.
We have been [INAUDIBLE] university.
Was very-- every year it was the same story.
What would happen to you?
Well, they would beat me up.
[LAUGHS]
Because I'm Jew.
[INAUDIBLE], he's a Jew, so that in the university, they have to change the place where I'm sitting, there's a Jewish section and a Polish section.
[NON-ENGLISH].
They didn't take [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] notice, or just go over this [INAUDIBLE] later.
They take them two, three weeks, and they [? think it ?] was quiet.
I-- I remember two-- one in [NON-ENGLISH], this Jewish section of Krakow.
And later, I was living in [NON-ENGLISH].
That was very close to my wife.
But that was by coincidence.
But there was another, not populated with Jew, not much, although maybe [INAUDIBLE].
[NON-ENGLISH].
[GERMAN], in German.
I would rather say no.
My father was observant, but I don't think that fanatic was.
I-- I was going only in high holidays.
The father was-- was taking us.
It was very small-- two rooms, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] opposite a very big synagogue, the high synagogue, we call it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was plenty of-- plenty of close friends, want to be in the shul, but it was-- it was in different surrounding, I believe.
Ambition to be a chairman or something.
No, there was no rabbi.
No, well, to say the truth, she was not.
Was this a source of conflict between your parents?
No, there was not a conflict.
My father accepted that one.
And my mother was coming to shul on the High Holy days.
But she was not observant.
Besides, that wasn't-- we-- we have a kosher house-- not, maybe, to the extent what they are doing now.
But it was a kosher.
Pardon?
Yes, I had a bar mitzvah, but only very-- very small, not-- not with a-- bar mitzvah [INAUDIBLE] now, now they have what's [? according to the ?] Torah.
And [? when ?] a very small kiddush, there was-- there's entertainment for-- was very simple.
You have a cake and wine, or something the same as [INAUDIBLE].
That was everything.
Rose-Dora.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, it was very nice.
I want to find-- we have been meeting, passing.
I was going from my-- you know, in Poland we have the main, in the mid-day, the supper what is here.
When I was going home from-- from home to the office, about 3 o'clock, she was coming from the school.
And I know her.
I met-- met his-- her brother, the oldest brother, in-- he was instructor-- ski instructor.
And I met-- met him, and we be close-- close friends.
But when I ask him to introduce me by his sister, she-- she was-- he was thinking that I meant his step-sister, and she was [INAUDIBLE].
A lot of my friends who-- who know her to introduce me to her.
She-- they didn't want me.
So once I would bring my cousin-- maybe second cousin, but not exactly cousin, named [NON-ENGLISH], to the dance [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the place was very popular for young people, and she was over there.
And I told him, you go home [INAUDIBLE], because I have [INAUDIBLE] just [? clop ?] my friend's [INAUDIBLE], and I had the dance.
And so that started.
And when was that?
When did you meet?
Oh, it was 1936, I believe-- '36.
Very much.
Very much.
Very much.
And did her family like you?
Pardon?
I was-- I know the-- the three brother of her, and they were very friendly with me.
I was [INAUDIBLE] in the same business, which she was-- he was making for Alexander, who is now living in Sydney.
He was making the same [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] business, what I was making, but in [INAUDIBLE] business.
So we know each other before I know [?
Ruja. ?]
On the 22nd of October, 1939.
There was nothing.
Rose-- [INAUDIBLE] my-- she had to push me to be married, because her brother went away when the war started, on a motorbike, to the east.
And she was by herself, and she told me it's not-- not good too-- too-- that I am by myself and not married.
So we decided to go have a Jewish wedding.
There was just-- in [NON-ENGLISH], there was [?
Kahal. ?] There was-- you know what is [?
Kahal ?] is?
And rabbi, he was a-- the wedding recite something.
But because my wife was not-- was orphan-- both parents was pass away-- so she has a trustee from the court.
Unfortunately, the-- the trustee was the oldest brother, who was away.

When she-- when we came to the court, the judge asked her whether that-- she is not [INAUDIBLE], or something of the kind, because he has some reservation because the trustee was in, was [INAUDIBLE] give her a [? go. ?] She-- by-- by chance, she find a partner of her sister-in-law in the court, and he was representing her.
I couldn't remember.
Yes.
I was a clerk-- clerk in-- in [INAUDIBLE] '37.
And later I went to the-- to-- to make-- my-- my duty as a-- in the military-- Polish military.
And when I come back, I get proposition to be presented from this other place.
[INAUDIBLE] Krakow district, that's all.
So to-- I was, rather, very free-- wherever I want to go, I just have to come afternoon, give the report to the-- or whether I have some orders, or inquiries, and so on.
Six-- 18 months.
I was have a very-- and there was a-- a chap who was-- going with my cousin for years.
And he was-- I believe he was a [INAUDIBLE].
It was the army who was fighting with the Australian-- Austrian people, against Russia.
And he has a connection.
I believe that he was doing that for money.
And he told me-- usually, the-- the duty was 24 months.
But when I-- and I have to go wherever they send me.
And I ask whether they could arrange for me to-- to be in Krakow.
And he told me, yes, and there is a one thing to have this-- then you'll be much better off, because you'll be only 18 months, or something of this kind.
What do you mean?
What job did you do, in the 18 months?

18 months, I was-- I don't know whether I call it-- just-- They want me to-- to take to the officer's school, but I don't want to, because I was not happy with it-- with all-- discipline and everything.
I was hating it.
What was your rank?
Just-- I don't know how to call it.
Just a soldier.
I didn't even accepted the capital, what they want from me, because you have to be leader, and leader have to be rather following the orders for-- for-- for the [INAUDIBLE] and so on.
No, I didn't agree with something, and I don't want to be the-- the leader.
I-- I was treated rather good, but there was-- in '35, some kind of [INAUDIBLE]-- some type-- some [INAUDIBLE], and there was very bad going on, things.
But one of the-- the-- they want to take [INAUDIBLE] will be, he was the commander of the 6th Division.
He took command of the military.

And there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a written [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what-- what soldier can do, what have to do, all that [? no. ?] Between that one was that Jewish people or people from the same state would-- would-- have to be the-- the army assistant can be excused from the duty.
So where we been staying in-- in front of the door, I have to step out three fee-- three feet, or three steps.
And this-- this [INAUDIBLE] who is-- who was the head of the company asked, what are you doing?
I ask-- I tell him, I am from Krakow.
I have to be excused.
The next answer was, Jewish and people who are from Krakow, step out.
What I [INAUDIBLE] that Jewish, even who was living in Krakow, have to step out.
So when did you leave the army?
In 1937-- 1937 is correct.
As in a small way.
I was a member of-- of Jewish Zionist organization--
--not very active.
But it's very attractive, because we have been going to excursions [INAUDIBLE].

Akiba. ?] And then later, when I was in high school, there was a-- another one, only I rather like [INAUDIBLE] the way to-- you know, by this time we change the books from the year, from-- from that higher class to the lower class.
And they have been trying to help financially, because we have been doing so.
And I couldn't remember that name.
[LAUGHS]
What kind of leader?
What kind of leader?
There was quite a few of them.
I remember Dr.
Ton, who was a rabbi, and a very progressive one.
He was member of parliament, in Poland parliament.
Dr.
Ton.
K-O-N.
T-- T-O-N.
[LAUGHS]
Ton.
Were you reading any newspapers?
Was there a Jewish newspaper?
Yes, there was [POLISH].
In Polish.
That was first of-- it was the 1st of September.
I was-- I was called to the army back in 15 of August.
The staff in our company couldn't believe that the war would be.
So they give us the rifles, only five ammunition.

And I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was sent to the patrol, to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] main station in Krakow, to-- to direct the people who were coming [INAUDIBLE], coming to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know how it is.
[INAUDIBLE] something or company, new companies, was [INAUDIBLE] company.
[INAUDIBLE] and I was-- I have 20 people with me.
And I said one, to take him away, but then he is coming back.
That was taking a few days-- rather, two days or three days.
On the 1st of September, I was in with another leader of patrol from second [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] air-- air-- air force in Krakow, in the trenches-- because was the sight of alarm.
And they--
OK.
OK.
